---
title: "The Unreasonable Effectiveness of Quarto"
subtitle: "Disentangling content from format in the age of AI agents"
author: "John Paul Helveston"
date: "posit::conf 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    transition: fade
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    slide-number: false
    history: false
---

<!--
  SCAFFOLD per HANDOFF.md §5 + approved plan. ~25 slides for ~18 min.
  Asset legend used in placeholders below:
    [SCREENSHOT], [PDF], [CSV], [RECORDING] = author supplies later
    [FIGURE] = entanglement diagram, lives in
               images/entanglement-diagram/entanglement-diagram.qmd
               (still being iterated; embed here once finalized)
  Build inline = SVG/HTML I produce directly.
-->

# How does an LLM make a document? {.inverse}

## {.inverse}

::: {.r-fit-text}
How does an LLM
<br>make a document?
:::

::: {.notes}
Philosophical walk-in. One line. The room knows Quarto, so take the slow road.
:::

## One probabilistic pass

Content and structure come out **together**, fused, in a single pass.

For a static one-off, that's fine.

The moment you want to *change* something, you're asking a probabilistic
system to edit a fused artifact — and hoping it touches only what you meant.

::: {.notes}
A small wink is fine here. Set up the fragility without naming Quarto yet.
:::

## The standard workflow

> **[FIGURE — State A (naive)]**
> `Me → LLM agent → output` (content + format fused).
> Only lever on the output is re-prompting the agent.
> Source: `images/entanglement-diagram/entanglement-diagram.qmd`, slide 1.

::: {.notes}
You prompt; it generates the whole finished output across the gap; all you can
do is review and re-prompt. One lever.
:::

## {.inverse}

::: {.r-fit-text}
LLMs entangle
<br>content and structure.
:::

[Neither is guaranteed.]{.font140 .orange}

::: {.notes}
Name the problem, stark. This is the thesis the rest of the talk answers.
:::

# Disentanglement {.inverse}

## Quarto in the middle

> **[FIGURE — State B (Quarto)]**
> Quarto sits in the gap: `.qmd` (structure) + `data files` (content).
> Agent writes only the `.qmd`. You gain direct edit arrows into both.
> **One lever became three.**
> Source: `images/entanglement-diagram/entanglement-diagram.qmd`, slide 2.

::: {.notes}
Same boxes, locked. The agent writes only the .qmd. .qmd + data render to the
output. The payoff: you reach past the agent and edit structure and content
directly.
:::

## A handwriting sheet for my 6-year-old

::: {.leftcol}
- A practice PDF: trace-the-sentence handwriting.
- Rendered `.qmd` → Typst → PDF.
- The *sentences* are the content that must be exact.
:::

::: {.rightcol}
> **[PDF — rendered handwriting sheet]**
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the emotional center. Make the abstract idea land for non-data people.
:::

## Ask the LLM for the PDF directly…

::: {.leftcol}
The sentences are only **probably** what you asked for.

Re-prompt to fix one word → the whole sheet regenerates, and anything could drift.
:::

::: {.rightcol}
> **[SCREENSHOT — directly-generated sheet, subtly wrong]**
:::

::: {.notes}
Probabilistic output: you can't point at a word and trust it survives the next edit.
:::

## …or put the sentences in a CSV

::: {.leftcol}
```r
# sentences.csv
The quick brown fox jumps.
A red kite flew over the hill.
We read books on rainy days.
```

The `.qmd` is structure. The CSV is content.
Editing a sentence is **editing a text file** — exact, every time.
:::

::: {.rightcol}
> **[PDF — same sheet, rendered from CSV]**
:::

::: {.notes}
Proves the point is about *sentences*, not just numbers. This is disentanglement
made concrete.
:::

# This is how you build anything {.inverse}

## The report whose data changed

::: {.leftcol}
A baked-in HTML report. The underlying numbers change.

The report is now **silently, confidently wrong** — nothing flags it.
:::

::: {.rightcol}
> **[SCREENSHOT — stale HTML report, wrong number highlighted]**
:::

::: {.notes}
HTML froze the numbers at generation time. No provenance, no recompute.
:::

## The `.qmd` just re-renders correctly

::: {.leftcol}
Same data change. Re-render.

The numbers are recomputed from source — **deterministically correct**.
:::

::: {.rightcol}
> **[SCREENSHOT — re-rendered report, correct number]**
:::

::: {.notes}
Same input change, opposite outcome. Correctness is the headline.
:::

## Modular by construction

[content]{.amber} in data files · [logic]{.blue} in chunks ·
[presentation]{.darkgreen} in YAML / themes

Each piece independently editable — by a human **or** an agent.

::: {.notes}
The architecture, generalized. Each layer is separable and auditable.
:::

## {.inverse}

::: {.r-fit-text}
HTML solves presentation.
<br>It does nothing for provenance.
:::

::: {.notes}
Bridge into Thariq. He's about to be right — about presentation.
:::

# Enter Thariq — and you give up nothing {.inverse}

## "The Unreasonable Effectiveness of HTML"

He's **right**: rich, structured output beats a wall of Markdown.

Agents should produce something you can *see and use*, not plain text.

::: {.notes}
Concede genuinely. We win by addition, not rebuttal. Paraphrase — no long quotes.
:::

## Quarto produces all of it

Everything HTML gives you — **plus** correctness and provenance that
HTML structurally cannot.

::: {.notes}
Reframe: not either/or. Quarto is a superset for anything that must stay correct.
:::

## Not a screenshot

> **[INLINE CHART — live interactive plot embedded in this slide]**
> (Observable Plot / plotly — author to pick data source)

This is rendered, interactive, **inside the deck**.

::: {.notes}
The "live in the slide" beat. It's itself evidence the approach works.
:::

## {.inverse}

::: {.r-fit-text}
This deck is
<br>a `.qmd` file.
:::

::: {.notes}
The meta-reveal. Let it sit.
:::

## A bonus: token efficiency

Same prompt, same model — only the output target changes:

| Asked for | Output tokens vs. `.qmd` |
|-----------|--------------------------|
| Word      | **7.2×** more            |
| HTML      | **4×** more              |
| PDF       | **1.8×** more            |

The `.qmd` request stays flat (~1,000 tokens) regardless of final format.

[Correctness is the headline. This is the bonus.]{.gray .small}

::: {.notes}
One supporting slide. Do NOT let tokens become the headline.
:::

## One honest concession

Throwaway, single-use custom editors?

That's **HTML's home turf** — concede it.

::: {.notes}
Buys credibility. We're not claiming Quarto wins everything.
:::

# The dashboard payoff {.inverse}

## A multi-page dashboard

> **[RECORDING — silent screen capture: Shinylive + JS libraries + React]**

::: {.notes}
The payoff image, not a deep dive. No audio.
:::

## The workflow

> **[FIGURE — human + Claude Code + Quarto loop]**

::: {.notes}
One diagram. Where the human, the agent, and deterministic rendering each sit.
:::

## Two real patterns

1. **[PATTERN — placeholder]**
2. **[PITFALL — placeholder]**

::: {.notes}
1–2 honest, lived pitfalls/patterns. Q&A ammo for the rest.
:::

# Close {.inverse}

## You don't need to know Quarto

Change **one word** in your prompt.

::: {.notes}
The accessibility close. This is for everyone, not just power users.
:::

## For power users

- Quarto **skills** / `CLAUDE.md` to steer the agent.
- **Lua filters** — there's even more under the hood.

::: {.notes}
Point the power users somewhere; tease depth without going there.
:::

## {.inverse}

::: {.r-fit-text}
Just add a `q`
<br>in front of `md`.
:::

::: {.notes}
Land it.
:::
